author: @Rei_Nori
text: I have enjoyed the pergola project. It helped me get out my head on my shop project, [I have been known to over-plan things a bit]. What I need in the workshop is heat in Winter. Frankly, it sucks going out to a 40°F shop that will take two hours to heat to a civil temp.  So a sand battery seems the obvious solution: No conversion to electricity loss. No complicated control. Cheap  (I am from a long line of Scotsmen who took crocodile wives; our arms are just too short to reach our wallets.)

author: @TheTubejunky
text: Pro tip: You can use a led light bulb (simple screw in type for lamps) to power some shop light leds that have had their power supply go up. I basically soldered the output wired of the bulb to the strings of leds in the shop light and it works great as well as saves more power.

author: @bigoptions
text: When you have a bunch of loads across a parallel light string, does the current from all the bulbs add up and change the voltage, or when would voltage of the return line change enough to affect the circuit. Maybe it would be just a limitation of how much current the return line can handle before it becomes another big load in the circuit. Different thicknesses and different lengths? Thanks! Also, have you seen the video about the simple problems with alternators in electric cars?

author: @Bill-n9l7d
text: Interestingly enough I have been trying to figure what to do with all these batteries in these disposable vapes. These batteries are still good when they go in the garbage. They are lithium ion batteries. So the thing is they are 3.8. volts. If I repurpose them a head lamp i use for working in the dark. I need 4.5 volts.I can stack two together but I'm over what is needed. So I am considering trying my hand at making an inductor coil type resistor out of that core wire in cable for TV's it's a copper alloy and should make a good resistor. Unless you can think of some way better. You could do a video. Those batteries need salvaging and regulation put in place ASAP mate have a wonderful day.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: To be honest mate to get what you want i would just stack two in series and add a voltage regulator

author: @willtucker2774
text:
At twelve volts, shorting the battery with a spanner, it becomes a fuse.even more troublesome at twenty four volts (trucks,buses and marine things)
Thats why i remove my watch and any rings.
Blow your finger off or your wrist.
Always remember, negative battery connection comes off first and goes on last.

But yeah, nice and simple setup.

author: @johnwynne-qx6br
text: Looks great 👍 I use those stepup/down boost converters for small projects and appart from one unit they have been great. I use a bigger version to run my 1920's radio from solar it has a cooling fan, fused and you can set amp draw so it clamps amperage to a range you choose, have been using it for year's and it's never got hot 🔥 and gives you peace of mind that you have fusses on battery aswell as the unit suppling the final output. Enjoy the sun energy robert it's a wonderful feeling every time you throw that switch and you know it isn't connected to a "rip off" meter 😂. 👍

author: @totherarf
text:
Yet another great video!
Have you checked out Kinetic Switches? They look like a normal house light switch but the energy you put in by flipping it on sends a pulse to a controller that will switch the 240 light on! Obviously there is no electrical connection to the actual switch.
I installed a set of LED under counter lights in my kitchen using this method. It allowed me to exercise my sense of humor by mounting the switch on the side of my Fridge/Freezer. It stuck on with a piece of double sided tape that is extremely strong and the switch is so low profile it looks like it is embedded!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's very cool mate - i hadn't heard of them - thanks for the heads up

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering YW

author: @anthonynorris7736
text: Is the efficiency of the dc power converter from 12v to 30v, compared to a ac power converter from a full 230v supply that down convert to the 30v Better or worse?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The dc-dc is more efficient mate

replies.author: @Mad4400
replies.text: Any conversion from AC 240v to DC will be less efficient compared with DC to DC conversion. Unfortunately, unless you are creating your own electricity, all power you use will be converted from AC at some point. In order to send electricity vast distances with minimal losses, it is converted into an AC signal. That's why grid power is AC.

author: @rafalklepinski7372
text:
Love DC-DC converters, just one tiny thing about the cheap ones outdoors.. that's basically a large antenna broadcasting switching harmonics that mess up AM receivers in a certain radius. I'd check an AM radio to see how bad things get before an angry neighbor or the equivalent of the FCC comes around with a fine ;) Those cheap converters don't give a toss about radio interference and I've had bad results with some in this respect.

I remember seeing a Youtube video about a manhunt for a radio interference source in the UK. They triangulated the signal to someones' lightbulb :) Don't recall if it was a CFL or an LED one. If a lightbulb peeved them off imagine this :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks mate - on a side note mine wasn't actually outdoors and i put it in a project box - so if it a worry just make the project box a Faraday cage - cheap and easy to do

replies.author: @Vincent_Sullivan
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Project box being a Faraday cage will not help unless you put suitable RF filtering on the DC input and output lines.  Rafal Klepinski is concerned about conducted radio frequency energy being radiated from the long wires connecting the DC to DC converter to your DC power source and DC load.

replies.author: @rafalklepinski7372
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It's the actual output wire and the light string that's acting as the antenna so a box won't help :) Fortunately this can be very simple to solve with an inductor in series and a capacitor in parallel on the output to act as a low-pass filter.

replies.author: @archibaldvonkranski8881
replies.text: Your over thinking this. I have an off grid system to run lights, radio and other devices all at once. Never had a problem with radio reception...

replies.author: @Vincent_Sullivan
replies.text: @archibaldvonkranski8881 Was that AM radio (0.5 to 1.5 MHz) or FM radio (88 to 108 MHz) that you were not having a problem with?  Frequency band and type of modulation makes a difference.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Great information. I am also amazed that it can handle 4 amps! Also the 0.5% regulation was pretty impressive for such a unit at that price. Very nice.
Oh I forgot to mention, that if you slightly underdrive your LEDs you will vastly improve their lifetime.

replies.author: @Иван_Абрамов
replies.text: The converter has a very old circuitry, and its efficiency is not high. I don't know where 4 amperes are stated, I understand it from the input, but I have big doubts that the module will be able to withstand a current of 2 amperes for a long time, not to mention 4 amperes, this is clearly not true. There are enough videos on the Internet about testing such modules, in the Ru segment for sure, these tests are very old, like these modules, but they all indicate a real long-term load of 1 ampere, especially taking into account the installation in an unventilated plastic box.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is what it says in the literature mate - but you might need to cool it to do that with it - but here it is only handling a third of an Amp so no worries

replies.author: @Иван_Абрамов
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I didn't talk about your case because you didn't say anything about the load. I told the person not to count on 4 amps, they are not here, I bought a couple dozen of such boards before, but by modern standards this is no longer a relevant board with low efficiency and not the best output in terms of pulsations, but for low-consumption bulbs - just right. Yes, you should be more critical of different things, including those written in literature. Texts are written by people, often not the most literate or biased, so there is a lot of untruth in literature, much more than truth.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @Иван_Абрамов Thanks for letting me know about the limitations.

replies.author: @paulie-g
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering These boards come from the same people who sell off-brand 18650s with 6000mAh ratings etc. It doesn't matter for your application (although my experience with landscape lighting is that you want short-circuit protection as well), but people need to understand that not all datasheets are created equal (and you almost certainly don't know if the markings on the chip are even genuine).

author: @FuchsDanin
text:
Well sure the XL6009 handles 4A MAXIMUM, and it outputs 50V MAXIMUM depending on the PCB you get it on, but DEFINITELY NOT both at the same time. Aliexpress and Amazon don't tell you the whole story. The LM6009 is only capable of ~25 watts without cooling, in an open-to-ambient case. In a small box, I'd push less than that. If you want more than ~25 watts safely, I suggest getting a more powerful boost controller.

Otherwise, good starter electronics info to have on hand. Good video. I enjoy your content.

replies.author: @wrathofsocrus
replies.text: Great advice. I've also found the tiny surface mount diode can get really hot and it's so small that adding cooling doesn't help much. The datasheet has a table of appropriate diodes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - the power load on these LEDs is 12 watts - so I am not particularly worried about it - but great advice.
